old_claim.note: State Cadastral Surveys provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Estate maps were large-scale maps resulting from surveying and measuring land; the scoped practice therefore depends directly on surveying rather than printed atlases or state cadastres.

source_shape.source_locator: History of Cartography chapter 28 defines estate maps as large-scale, usually manuscript maps resulting from surveying and measuring land.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source explains estate maps as products of land surveying and measurement for rural land management.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports surveying as the direct enabling method; state cadastral surveys are a different public administrative mapping tradition and are not required for private estate maps.

ontology_before: Estate Survey Maps depended on State Cadastral Surveys, implying private estate maps derived from large-scale state cadastral programs.
ontology_after: Estate Survey Maps depend directly on surveying as the source-backed measuring practice that produced private estate maps.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: estate_survey_maps now depends on surveying and no longer directly depends on surveying_cadastral_states.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to state cadastral surveys rather than private estate maps.
A stronger source showed early estate maps required a state cadastral survey program before they could be produced.
The surveying node were narrowed away from land measurement and boundary surveying.
short_reason: Surveying is the source-backed method for producing estate maps.
